title: Eighty percent of care, one percent of evidence: quantifying the nursing trial deficit in Africa
body: Nurses deliver an estimated 80% or more of healthcare in Africa, yet the evidence base for nursing interventions on the continent is critically thin. We queried ClinicalTrials.gov API v2 for trials across eight nursing categories -- general nursing, midwifery, wound care, infection control, patient education, palliative nursing, task-shifting, and maternal/neonatal nursing -- in Africa versus the United States and United Kingdom. Africa had 347 nursing/nurse-led trials compared with 2,861 in the US (8.2x ratio). Nursing trial density (percentage of all trials involving nursing) was 1.81% in Africa versus 1.8% in the US and 0.98% in the UK. Midwifery trials (192 in Africa) were critically scarce given that sub-Saharan Africa accounts for 70% of global maternal deaths. Wound care (1,765), infection control (3,503), and palliative nursing (114) trials were minimal. Task-shifting to nurses (79 trials) represents an area where African experience is globally relevant. The evidence-care mismatch is stark: those who deliver most of Africa's healthcare have almost none of the evidence to guide their practice. This analysis is limited to one registry and keyword-based trial identification.

sentences.text: What proportion of clinical trial evidence in Africa addresses nursing interventions, given that nurses deliver the vast majority of healthcare on the continent?
We queried ClinicalTrials.gov API v2 for trials across eight nursing categories in Africa, comparing with the United States and United Kingdom as benchmarks.
Africa had 347 nursing trials versus 2,861 in the US, with nursing trial density of 1.81% in Africa compared with 1.8% in the US.
Midwifery trials (192 in Africa vs 1,044 in the US) were critically scarce for a region accounting for 70% of global maternal deaths.
Wound care (1,765), infection control (3,503), and palliative nursing (114) trials were minimal, leaving nurses to manage these fundamental areas without locally generated evidence.

The evidence-care mismatch in African nursing is profound: the workforce delivering 80% of care receives approximately 1% of research attention, perpetuating practice based on protocols designed for fundamentally different health systems.

This analysis is limited to ClinicalTrials.gov, uses keyword-based identification that may miss embedded nursing components, and does not capture quality improvement projects or observational studies.

outsideNote.data: ClinicalTrials.gov API v2, 8 nursing categories, Africa vs US/UK

ai_transparency: LLM assistance was used for drafting and language editing. The author reviewed and edited the manuscript and takes responsibility for the final content.
